Article Title: After losing White House's trust, Anthropic changes things; making White House now happy talking to Dario Amodei's company as ...
Article Text:
How and why Anthropic lost the White House's trust



The Trump administration’s relationship with artificial intelligence (AI) startup Anthropic is reportedly beginning to improve. Citing sources familiar with the matter, a report by Wired claims that the ‘breakthrough’ comes after a major internal shakeup in who represents the company in high-stakes government meetings.The report says that White House officials are much happier dealing with Anthropic lately for a simple reason: they no longer have to negotiate with CEO Dario Amodei, who has been replaced in critical diplomatic meetings by co-founder, Tom Brown, who is said to be trying to salvage the release of the company’s highly anticipated “Claude Fable 5” AI model.“Tom Brown is not being a weirdo like Dario and can actually engage,” one person familiar with the recent calls was quoted as saying, noting that the administration previously found Amodei too difficult to talk to and dismissive of national security concerns.The tension between the tech firm and Washington peaked on June 12, 2026, when the US government deployed strict export controls that forced Anthropic’s most powerful models offline. The restriction occurred after the National Security Agency (NSA) proved that foreign actors could easily disable the software’s built-in guardrails to access ‘dangerously powerful’ capabilities hidden within Anthropic's restricted “Mythos” model.However, a report by The Wall Street Journal reported that a breach of trust had been brewing weeks before the official shutdown.Citing officials speaking on the condition of anonymity, Anthropic originally provided the administration with a strict list of 111 trusted global organisations approved to receive advanced access to the Mythos model. Given that Anthropic itself warned the tech could dramatically supercharge the capabilities of rogue hackers, US officials reviewed and signed off on the list.Nevertheless, the relationship shattered when Anthropic later quietly disclosed that the list had ballooned, giving roughly 50 unapproved foreign entities access to the software without government knowledge. When Anthropic revealed the identities, the White House discovered that one of the secret recipients was a South Korean telecommunications firm heavily suspected of maintaining back-channel ties to China.While Anthropic moving quickly to revoke the South Korean firm's access stopped a wider geopolitical crisis, the damage to the company's reputation was already done. “They expanded it too far and wide,” a White House official was quoted as saying, noting the episode completely ruined their confidence in Anthropic’s ability to protect sensitive American tech.
Article Summary: Anthropic's CEO Dario Amodei has been replaced in diplomatic meetings by co- founder, Tom Brown. The tension between the tech firm and Washington peaked on June 12, 2026, when the US government deployed strict export controls that forced Anthropic’s most powerful models offline.

Article Title: A suitcase-sized battery in the closet could soon save homeowners money, as rapid growth in home energy storage could ease pressure on power grids while cutting emissions, researchers say
Article Text:
Homeowners are increasingly adopting compact battery systems, transforming their houses into smart energy hubs. Image Credit: Chat GPT

Technology: Battery storage – Global Energy Review 2026

The silent workhorse of the modern clean energy home

These systems offer backup power and intelligently manage electricity use by charging when prices are low and discharging during peak times, significantly reducing energy bills. Image Credit: Chat GPT

Redefining the relationship between households and the grid

Around our homes, quiet design revolutions have been changing daily life on a small scale. Bulky desktops have given way to tablets, and large entertainment systems to wall-mounted flat screens. While other innovations have advanced, the equipment that powers daily life has changed little and often sits in a basement or utility room. However, a new wave of innovation has begun to sweep through the field.A compact, suitcase-sized battery inside a hallway closet or mounted to a garage wall is quickly becoming a useful household asset for suburban homeowners. These home energy systems can provide backup power and help manage electricity use based on market conditions. By charging when power is cheap and abundant, and discharging when prices spike, these compact units can help families reduce exposure to volatile energy bills while managing their electricity use.This massive global shift in clean tech deployment is highlighted in a market analysis published by the International Energy Agency titled. The report says battery storage is among the fastest-growing power technologies today.According to the data, global storage capacity additions rose sharply, adding 108 gigawatts worldwide. This rapid expansion means the total global installed storage base is now about 11 times its level from four years ago.To understand why these compact domestic systems are spreading so rapidly through neighbourhoods across the United Kingdom, the United States, Germany, and Australia, it helps to look at the hidden economic incentives driving the clean tech lifestyle. Historically, homeowners with rooftop solar panels often had to use the electricity as it was generated or sell it back to the grid for a modest return. Home batteries change this dynamic by letting families store solar power for use later in the day, when household demand peaks.The data in the Global Energy Review 2026 points to a dramatic technological consolidation that is making these domestic installations much more affordable and practical for everyday consumers. The analysis highlights that lithium iron phosphate batteries have captured a large share of new storage deployments worldwide. While this battery chemistry is less energy-dense than some EV batteries, it is generally cheaper to produce and well-suited to frequent daily charging cycles.This technological evolution is providing a crucial lifeline to regional utility networks that are struggling to balance the unpredictable nature of wind and solar power generation. While large utility-scale installations made up most of the new storage capacity added to the world's grids, the rest came from behind-the-meter systems. By absorbing excess power during sunny afternoons and easing strain on local infrastructure during peak periods, household batteries can help balance electrical systems.The rapid adoption of these smart home systems is changing the relationship between households and energy suppliers. In standard electricity markets, consumers often pay rates set by their utility and can still be affected by fuel supply shocks or seasonal spikes in demand. With an intelligent storage system, a modern home can become more active in managing when it uses grid power.As more countries adopt flexible, time-of-use pricing models, the financial case for home batteries is becoming stronger. Automated software can analyse weather forecasts, household usage patterns and real-time electricity rates to decide when to draw power from the grid or use stored reserves. This balancing act can help homeowners manage major appliances during peak, expensive hoursThe clean technology transition is not happening only in remote deserts where large solar farms are built. This transformation is also happening at the household level, driven by economic and energy-security concerns. Using home battery technology can help make the grid more flexible, though its climate impact depends on how the electricity is generated.
Article Summary: Homeowners are increasingly adopting compact battery systems, transforming their houses into smart energy hubs. The rapid adoption of these smart home systems is changing the relationship between households and energy suppliers. Using home battery technology can help make the grid more flexible, though its climate impact depends on how the electricity is generated.

Article Title: AI emerges as key growth driver for MSMEs, Vodafone Idea study finds
Article Text:
Key findings



Cybersecurity adoption: 46.3% of MSMEs have adopted cyber defence solutions to counter rising online threats.

Women-led MSMEs: Achieved a DMI of 61.6, outperforming male-led businesses at 60.4.

Digital Workplace: Fastest-growing pillar, rising from 57.5 in 2023 to 65.7 in 2026, driven by collaboration platforms and HRMS solutions.

Digital Operations: Improved from 56.5 to 62.0, reflecting stronger process efficiency and resilience.

Financial readiness introduced



Sectoral and regional highlights



Financial Services emerged as the most digitally mature sector (DMI 67.3), overtaking IT & ITeS (66.2).

Retail recorded the strongest improvement, rising 9.9 points to 61.9, driven by omnichannel adoption and digital payments.

Tourism & Hospitality, Energy & Utilities, and Transportation also showed strong gains.

Eastern states continue to lag, with no representation in the top 10 digitally mature MSME ecosystems.

India’s MSME sector, which contributes over 31% of GDP, is accelerating its digital adoption, with AI emerging as a key driver of scale. The latest Vi Business MSME Growth Insights Study 2026 reveals that 57% of MSMEs view AI as a vital tool for business growth, while 25% have already deployed AI solutions in their workflows. The study, based on Vi Business’s ReadyForNext platform that engaged over 2.5 lakh MSMEs across 16 sectors, shows India’s Digital Maturity Index (DMI) rising to 60.8 in 2026, up from 58.0 in 2025. Telangana (68.3) and Karnataka (65.7) lead state rankings, while Andhra Pradesh entered the top 10 for the first time.Vi Business has expanded its assessment framework by adding Financial Readiness as the fourth pillar, alongside Digital Customer, Digital Workplace, and Digital Operations. This new benchmark will evaluate MSMEs’ funding preparedness and risk coverage awareness, mapping how effectively digital adoption translates into financial strength.M P Sunil Kumar, Chief Enterprise Business Officer at Vodafone Idea, said: “India’s MSMEs are moving beyond basic digitisation towards building intelligent, secure, and future-ready enterprises. By introducing the Financial Readiness Pillar, we aim to provide an ecosystem blueprint that helps small and medium businesses evaluate resilience, secure funding, and scale sustainably.”
Article Summary: Financial Services emerged as the most digitally mature sector (DMI 67.3), overtaking IT & ITeS (66.2) Telangana (68.3) and Karnataka (65.7) lead state rankings, while Andhra Pradesh entered the top 10 for the first time. 46.3% of MSMEs have adopted cyber defence solutions to counter rising online threats.

Article Title: After 'banning' Anthropic’s AI, US government now sends message to OpenAI CEO Sam Altman; says: Your new AI model can only be available to…
Article Text:
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman agreed to a limited GPT-5.6 release after the Trump administration asked the company to restrict access to government-approved partners first.

A softer landing than the one Anthropic got

Washington’s new rulebook for AI is still being written

Why US government suddenly cares so much about AI launches

The US government has asked OpenAI to hold back its newest AI model, GPT-5.6, releasing it only to a small group of partners that Washington signs off on first, according to a report by The Information. The move comes barely two weeks after the same administration forced Anthropic to yank its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models offline entirely, signaling that the White House is now actively policing how the most powerful AI systems reach the public. CEO Sam Altman told staff the controlled rollout wasn't OpenAI's "preferred" approach, but the company agreed to it anyway, with federal officials set to approve customer access one by one during the preview window.The request came from the White House's Office of the National Cyber Director and the Office of Science and Technology Policy, with Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick also in the loop. Altman reportedly discussed GPT-5.6 directly with Lutnick on Wednesday, and the Commerce chief wanted assurance that every relevant arm of the government had tested and cleared the model before any launch. The Information first surfaced the plan, citing a leaked internal memo Altman sent to employees.What stands out is how differently the two companies have been treated. When Anthropic shipped Fable 5 on June 9, it took just three days for the government to step in. By the evening of June 12, Commerce had issued an export control directive barring any foreign national, including Anthropic's own non-citizen employees, from touching Fable 5 or Mythos 5. The net was so wide that Anthropic had no choice but to switch off both models for everyone. The company was reportedly handed a 90-minute window to comply.OpenAI, by contrast, gets to keep its model available, just to a vetted list. Sources told Axios the company had been working with the administration on the release well before the Anthropic blowup, which likely smoothed the path. One source framed the intervention plainly: GPT-5.6 carries "Mythos-like" capability, and that level of power is what triggered the extra scrutiny rather than any sudden policy shift.All of this traces back to an executive order Trump signed on June 2, which asks AI labs to give the government up to 30 days to evaluate powerful new models before release. The catch is that the order is officially voluntary and the actual review framework doesn't have to be finished until the end of July. So companies are navigating a process whose rules keep shifting and, by some accounts, aren't fully settled even inside the administration. Dean Ball, a former Trump AI policy adviser who recently joined OpenAI, captured the mood bluntly, noting that the requirements change constantly and stay secret even to the officials applying them.That uncertainty has spread across the industry. The government is separately pressing Meta, the lone major holdout, to submit its models for review. OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI and Microsoft have all already agreed to share their systems with the Center for AI Standards and Innovation, the Commerce-housed group Lutnick oversees.The deeper worry driving all this is cybersecurity. Officials are increasingly anxious about what happens when nation-state hackers, cybercriminals or rogue insiders get hold of frontier models capable of finding and exploiting software flaws. That same fear is what sank Fable 5, after Amazon researchers showed they could coax the model into revealing security vulnerabilities. Critics argued the panic was overblown, since rival models, including OpenAI's own, can do much the same.For now, Altman says he hopes to push GPT-5.6 to a wider audience just a "couple of weeks" after the limited preview. Whether that timeline holds depends on a review process the government is still building in real time.
Article Summary: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 will be limited to government-approved partners first. The move comes barely two weeks after Anthropic was forced to pull its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models. The White House is now actively policing how the most powerful AI systems reach the public.

Article Title: New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani files motion to ban delivery app Motoclick; lawsuit says: Paying New York delivery workers poverty wages is ...
Article Text:
New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani (AP Photo/Heather Khalifa)

What is the NYC vs Motoclick case all about

New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani wants to ban the food delivery platform Motoclick after it reportedly admitted to paying workers less than a quarter of New York City’s required delivery worker minimum wage, city officials told a federal judge this week. New York Commissioner Samuel Levine, the New York City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP), Corporation Counsel Steve Banks, and the New York City Law Department filed a motion seeking a preliminary injunction to stop Patio Delivery Inc and its agents from operating in New York City unless they come into compliance with the City’s Delivery Worker Laws by paying workers the $22.13 per hour they are owed under the minimum pay rate. In a release, NYC government claimed that this is the first time DCWP has sought a preliminary injunction to protect delivery workers.The City’s lawsuit filed against Motoclick in January alleging egregious violations of the Delivery Worker Laws seeks millions in missing pay and damages. The filing alleges that Motoclick produced reports to DCWP showing that it paid workers between $3.67 and $4.67 per hour in the month of May, a fraction of the lawful minimum pay rate.This new enforcement action seeks to bar the company from operating in New York City unless it begins paying workers a lawful rate.“Paying New York delivery workers poverty wages is not only wrong—it's illegal,” said Commissioner Samuel A.A. Levine. “DCWP is asking the Court to shut Motoclick down unless and until it comes into compliance. New York will not allow this company to continue underpaying New Yorkers.”“Paying hardworking delivery workers as little as three to four dollars an hour is not only morally wrong, it’s illegal,” said New York City Corporation Counsel Steve Banks. “As our legal action makes clear, the City of New York will not tolerate companies that abuse their workers and violate our local laws.”“Delivery workers fought for years to win New York City’s minimum pay protections, and those rights only have meaning when they are enforced,” said Ligia Guallpa, Executive Director of Worker’s Justice Project and Co-Founder of its Los Deliveristas Unidos. “We are grateful to Commissioner Levine, the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection, and the City Law Department for taking decisive action to hold Motoclick accountable for some of the most brazen alleged wage theft we have seen in this industry. No worker should be earning three or four dollars an hour while delivery companies profit from their labor. This action sends a clear message that companies cannot evade the law, undercut responsible businesses, or exploit workers with impunity. It also shows why New York is leading the nation in ensuring that every delivery worker receives the pay they have earned and deserve.”In January, the City filed a case in court against Motoclick, alleging that the company has been egregiously violating the city’s Delivery Worker Laws by paying workers below the legal minimum pay rate and deducting fees from workers’ pay. DCWP estimates that Motoclick and its CEO Juan Pablo Salinas Salek owe workers millions in back pay. The case is in the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York, City of New York v. Patio Delivery Inc., No. 26-CV-01287.Third-party food and grocery delivery apps are required to submit monthly data reports so that DCWP can monitor compliance with the Minimum Pay Rule. In May, Motoclick, which operates a food delivery app, provided reports to DCWP that show violations of the law. For example, Motoclick’s May 2026 report shows illegally low pay of $4.67 per hour, or even less, while its April report shows that workers were underpaid by $172,010.67 in a single week.The City's pioneering Minimum Pay Rate rule, established by Local Law 115 of 2021, dramatically increased average hourly earnings for app-based delivery workers without reducing deliveries. In April, the MPR increased to $22.13, reflecting a 3.2% adjustment for inflation.DCWP urges all workers who believe their rights have been violated to file a complaint by visiting nyc. gov/workers. Since the start of the Mamdani administration, DCWP has secured more than $9.3 million in restitution for New York City consumers, workers, and small businesses, including nearly $5 million for Uber Eats, Fantuan and HungryPanda delivery workers.DCWP's lead attorneys on the case are Supervising Enforcement Counsel John De Vito, Staff Counsel Gabriel Gutierrez, and Staff Counsel Christopher Taylor of DCWP's Office of Labor Policy & Standards, under the supervision of Litigation Director Emily Hoffman and Deputy Commissioner Elizabeth Wagoner.
Article Summary: New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani wants to ban the food delivery platform Motoclick after it reportedly admitted to paying workers less than a quarter of New York City’s required delivery worker minimum wage. New York Commissioner Samuel Levine, the New York. City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP), Corporation. Counsel Steve Banks, and the New. York City Law Department filed a motion seeking a preliminary injunction to stop Patio Delivery Inc and. its agents from operating.

Article Title: Facebook's ex-director Sarah Wynn-Williams and author of the ‘explosive’ book that claimed ex-COO Sheryl Sandberg asked assistant to 'come to bed' sues Meta over Gag order
Article Text:
Sarah Wynn-Williams

‘Come to bed’: What Sarah Wynn-Williams claimed in memoir



Meta responds, says: Former employee wants to sell her book



Sarah Wynn-Williams, a former Facebook policy executive, has filed a lawsuit against Mark Zuckerberg's Meta over an arbitration order that has legally silenced her for more than a year. Wynn-Williams, who is the author of “Careless People: A Cautionary Tale of Power, Greed, and Lost Idealism” and made controversial claims targeting top leadership, filed the lawsuit in the US District Court for the Northern District of California, alleging that Meta is using an invalid arbitration agreement to enforce a gag order.According to a report by The New York Post, the company is reportedly pursuing catastrophic financial penalties of $50,000 for each alleged violation, including every single copy of her book sold.Published in March 2025, the memoir details Wynn-Williams’ six-year tenure at the tech giant before she was fired in 2017. The book contains highly controversial claims targeting top leadership, depicting what she describes as a “rotten” corporate culture.Among the most sensational claims in the book are allegations leveled against former COO Sheryl Sandberg. Wynn-Williams writes that during a corporate trip to Europe, Sandberg spent $13,000 on lingerie for herself and a young female assistant.She further alleges that during a flight home on a private jet, Sandberg asked the assistant to join her in “the only bed on the plane.”Furthemore, the book also targets Meta’s current policy chief and president of global affairs, Joel Kaplan, who previously supervised Wynn-Williams. She accuses Kaplan of sexual harassment, alleging he pressed against her on the dance floor during a corporate event and called her “sultry.”When it comes to CEO Mark Zuckerberg, she claimed that he worked “hand in glove” with Beijing and considered sharing Facebook user data with the China in exchange for access to the restricted Chinese market.Meta has rejected all of Wynn-Williams’ claims, labeling the book as false and defamatory. The company states that Wynn-Williams was terminated nearly a decade ago for “poor performance and toxic behaviour,” and that an internal investigation at the time cleared Kaplan of any wrongdoing after finding she had made false claims against colleagues.“This former employee is trying to use the legal process to sell books, which an arbitrator already ruled broke the agreement she signed with the company when she accepted a large severance payment years ago,” a Meta spokesperson told the publication.The tech giant recently scored a major legal victory when an independent arbitrator ruled in Meta's favor, extending the restrictions to Macmillan Publishers and Flatiron Books.“This ruling affirms that Sarah Wynn-Williams’ false and defamatory book should never have been published. This urgent legal action was made necessary by Williams, who... deliberately concealed the existence of her book project and avoided the industry’s standard fact-checking process in order to rush it to shelves,” Meta spokesperson Andy Stone had said on X at that time.In her latest lawsuit to lift the gag order and void her severance agreement, Wynn-Williams claims she was financially and emotionally coerced into signing the original non-disparagement and mandatory arbitration clauses when she was terminated.
Article Summary: Sarah Wynn-Williams, a former Facebook policy executive, has filed a lawsuit against Mark Zuckerberg's Meta. She claims Meta is using an invalid arbitration agreement to enforce a gag order. The company is reportedly pursuing catastrophic financial penalties of $50,000 for each alleged violation, including every single copy of her book sold.

Article Title: Oracle founder Larry Ellison's $45 million-plus 'quite donation' to nonprofits and programmes backing Trump 'revealed' and how it runs counter to the donations from other billionaires
Article Text:
Counter donations made by other billionaires



Strategic wins for Oracle



White House response



Larry Ellison’s Oracle gets contract for US government



Larry Ellison, the founder of Oracle and the world’s sixth-richest man has quietly donated more than $45 million to nonprofits and programs backing US President Donald Trump, according to a report by The Wall Street Journal. The funding, not subject to disclosure rules, has not been publicly reported until this week. Ellison’s low-profile contributions stand in contrast to other billionaires who have openly aligned themselves with Trump through public appearances, White House dinners and other high-profile events.While tech leaders such as Elon Musk, Tim Cook, Kelly Ortberg, and Jensen Huang joined Trump on his recent trip to China, and executives like Mark Zuckerberg and Alexis Ohanian attended his 80th birthday celebration, Ellison was notably absent. Instead, he has continued funneling millions into groups supporting Trump since the 2024 election, with Oracle also listed as a corporate sponsor for Freedom 250.The quiet backing of Ellison has coincided with major wins for Oracle. The day after Trump’s second inauguration, Oracle was named as anchor of Stargate, a $500 billion plan to build AI data centers across the US. Later in 2025, Oracle joined the consortium that took over TikTok’s U.S.operations after Trump cleared the deal by executive order. Oracle’s performance obligations surged to $455 billion, up 359% year over year.Ellison’s family has also benefited. His son David Ellison’s company, Skydance, merged with Paramount after Trump’s administration cleared the deal. More recently, regulators approved Skydance’s takeover of Warner Bros. Discovery, handing the family control of CNN a network Ellison has long criticized.The White House told Fortune that Trump is not playing favorites. “President Trump is committed to working with every American business and business leader to cement America’s innovative dominance, reshore critical manufacturing, and accelerate economic growth for everyday American workers,” spokesman Kush Desai said.Recently, The Trump administration awarded Oracle a massive contract to build a unified, government-wide human resources platform. The Office of Personnel Management (OPM), which acts as the federal government's central HR department, selected the tech giant for the 10-year, $395.8 million “Federal HR 2.0” contract.According to reports by news agency Reuters and local publication Washington Technology, Oracle, whose executive chairman Larry Ellison is a prominent Trump supporter and member of the president’s science and technology council, beat out heavy competition for the deal.The cloud-based platform will be tasked with managing human resource functions for more than two million federal employees. The tech giant faced off against rival software and consulting heavyweights, including Workday, IBM, SAP, and Economic Systems Inc.
Article Summary: Ellison, the founder of Oracle and the world’s sixth-richest man has quietly donated more than $45 million to nonprofits and programs backing US President Donald Trump. The funding, not subject to disclosure rules, has not been publicly reported until this week. Ellison's low-profile contributions stand in contrast to other billionaires who have openly aligned themselves with Trump.

Article Title: Apple’s MacBook, iPad price hike is an AI warning users can’t ignore
Article Text:
AI is consuming the world's memory chips

Why other gadget makers too have little choice

India may feel the impact even more sharply.



Apple’s steep increase in MacBook and iPad prices in India is more than a premium brand charging premium prices. It is perhaps the clearest sign yet that the artificial intelligence boom is beginning to reshape consumer electronics in ways buyers will actually notice — through their wallets.The company has raised prices across much of its Mac and iPad portfolio after citing an unprecedented spike in memory and storage component costs. The biggest jump has come in the MacBook Pro lineup, where some models now cost Rs 70,000 more than they did at launch. While Apple has been the first major company to pass these costs on to Indian consumers, it is unlikely to remain the last.What looks like an isolated pricing decision today could soon become an industry-wide trend.The irony is hard to miss. Artificial intelligence was supposed to make computers smarter. Instead, it is making them more expensive.The explosion in demand for AI infrastructure has created an extraordinary appetite for high-bandwidth memory (HBM), DRAM and NAND flash storage. Every large AI cluster built by companies such as OpenAI, Microsoft, Google, Meta and Amazon requires vast quantities of cutting-edge memory chips.These chips have become the new bottleneck in the AI supply chain.Manufacturers including Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron have prioritised production of high-margin enterprise memory destined for AI servers. That leaves fewer chips for laptops, tablets and other consumer devices.The result is simple economics. Limited supply and soaring demand have pushed memory prices sharply higher over the past year. Apple, which ships millions of Macs and iPads globally, is now paying significantly more for the components that power every device.Unlike temporary spikes caused by logistics disruptions or currency movements, this shortage is structural. AI data centres continue to expand at an unprecedented pace, and chipmakers cannot add new manufacturing capacity overnight. New fabs cost billions of dollars and often take years before they begin commercial production.That means component inflation may not disappear anytime soon.Apple has effectively acknowledged this reality. Instead of absorbing the additional cost, it has passed a substantial portion to customers.The implications stretch far beyond Cupertino.Apple occupies a unique position in the PC industry. Its loyal customer base gives it greater flexibility to raise prices than most rivals. But the underlying cost pressures affect everyone buying memory and storage chips.That is why executives across the broader PC ecosystem have been quietly warning about higher prices for months.For Indian consumers, that could mean the era of steadily falling laptop prices is coming to an end.The timing is particularly significant. The PC market has only recently emerged from one of its weakest periods in years. Manufacturers had relied on aggressive discounts to stimulate demand after the post-pandemic slowdown. AI is now reversing that equation. Instead of excess inventory driving discounts, scarce components are driving price increases.The country imports the overwhelming majority of premium laptops and tablets, making retail prices vulnerable not only to global component costs but also to currency fluctuations, freight charges and import-related expenses. Every increase in memory costs gets amplified by the time a finished device reaches Indian shelves.Consumers looking to upgrade over the next year may therefore face difficult choices. Either stretch budgets for the same specifications or settle for lower memory and storage configurations that could age faster in an AI-first computing era.Ironically, AI itself is increasing the need for more capable hardware. New AI features in Windows PCs, macOS and productivity applications increasingly demand higher RAM and faster storage. Buyers are therefore being nudged towards more expensive configurations precisely when those configurations are becoming costlier to manufacture.This creates a rare double squeeze: consumers need more powerful machines, while the components required to build them are becoming scarcer and more expensive.The broader lesson is that AI's economic impact is no longer confined to cloud companies spending billions on GPUs. It is now spilling into everyday consumer technology.For years, smartphones and laptops became faster while gradually becoming better value. The AI era may reverse that long-standing trend. Computing power will continue to improve, but affordability may no longer keep pace.Apple's latest price hike is unlikely to be remembered simply as another revision to a premium product lineup. It could mark the moment when India's electronics market began paying the hidden cost of the global AI race.If memory chips have become the new oil of the digital economy, consumers are now discovering who ultimately foots the bill.
Article Summary: Apple has raised prices across much of its Mac and iPad portfolio after citing an unprecedented spike in memory and storage component costs. The biggest jump has come in the MacBook Pro lineup, where some models now cost Rs 70,000 more than they did at launch. Apple has been the first major company to pass these costs on to Indian consumers.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Sundar Pichai: "Wear your failure as a badge of…" - why the Google CEO wants you to stop hiding your setbacks
Article Text:
Sundar Pichai

Quote of the day by Google CEO Sundar Pichai

"Wear your failure as a badge of honour."

The story behind the quote by Sundar Pichai

What is the meaning of the quote by Sundar Pichai

Why this quote by Sundar Pichai is relevant

How to apply this quote in daily life

Reframe your failures as evidence you tried. When something does not work out, remind yourself it means you took a real risk, which most people quietly avoid.

Talk about your failures openly. Sharing what went wrong, and what you learned, takes away their power to embarrass you and often helps others too.

Let go of the fear of looking foolish. Most worthwhile things carry some chance of failure. Accepting that in advance makes it easier to begin.

Always pull a lesson from the stumble. A failure worn as a badge of honour is one you actually learned from. Ask what it taught you, then carry that into the next attempt.

Other famous quotes by Sundar Pichai



"It is always good to work with people who make you feel insecure about yourself. That way, you will constantly keep pushing your limits."

"We try to work on things which billions of people will use every day."

"My parents sacrificed a lot of their life and a lot of their disposable income to make sure their children were educated."

Most of us are taught to hide our failures. We tuck them away, leave them off our resumes, and quietly hope nobody asks. Sundar Pichai , the man who runs Google, suggests doing almost the exact opposite. Wear your failure as a badge of honour, he told a hall full of students. A badge of honour is not something you hide. It is something you pin to your chest and wear with pride, a mark that you did something difficult or brave. Pichai was saying that failure, far from being shameful, is proof that you tried, that you took a risk and dared to attempt something that might not work. In just a handful of words, he turns one of our most common fears on its head. Instead of treating failure as the opposite of success, he treats it as part of the path toward it.Pichai said this during a visit to India in December 2015, not long after becoming Google's chief executive, while speaking to a gathering of students. The talk was full of down to earth advice for young people just starting out.He encouraged them to reinvent themselves, to take risks, and to think big and chase work that genuinely excited them, even if it might not succeed.It was in that spirit that he offered the line about failure. His message was simple. Do not let the fear of failing stop you from attempting something worthwhile, and if you do fail, do not bury it. Wear it, learn from it, and try again.The power of the quote lies in the image of a badge of honour. A badge of honour is something earned and displayed with pride, often for bravery or service. By comparing failure to such a badge, Pichai flips its usual meaning completely.Failure is normally something we feel embarrassed about and want to bury. He is suggesting it should instead be seen as evidence of courage, the natural mark of anyone willing to take real risks and try hard things. You cannot fail at something you never dared to attempt. So in his view, a history of failures is not a list of embarrassments. It is proof that you kept putting yourself out there, which is something most people are too cautious to do.This matters because fear of failure holds so many people back. We talk ourselves out of applying, starting, asking or trying, simply because we might not succeed and might look foolish in front of others. That fear is often a far bigger obstacle than failure itself ever turns out to be.Pichai's line is a direct answer to it. If failure is a badge of honour rather than a source of shame, then there is much less reason to avoid the risk to begin with. The idea is especially fitting in a world that moves quickly and rewards those willing to experiment. Almost everyone who has built something worthwhile has a trail of failures behind them. They simply chose not to be defined by the stumbles.You do not need to run a company to take this advice on board. It is really about how you treat your own setbacks.There is something reassuring about hearing this from someone at the very top of the corporate world. Pichai is not pretending failure is fun, or that it does not sting. He is saying it is nothing to be ashamed of, and that the people who achieve the most are usually the ones who were willing to risk failing in the first place. The next time something you attempt does not work out, you can treat it as a wound to hide, or as a badge you have earned. Pichai's advice is to pin it on and keep going.
Article Summary: Sundar Pichai said this during a visit to India in December 2015. He was speaking to a gathering of students. He encouraged them to reinvent themselves, to take risks, and to chase work that genuinely excited them. The power of the quote lies in the image of a badge of honour.

Article Title: In 1986, three scientists improved backpropagation, making neural networks learn effectively from data
Article Text:
Geoffrey Hinton at the 2024 Nobel Prize press conference at Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Why early AI learning systems failed

A neural network with multiple layers. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Sending errors backward through the network

The long road to the current technology revolution

Each time you see your face recognised by a modern computer, or get an answer to a difficult question by a computer program that can translate languages, there is an important mathematical technique behind it. For many years, scientists knew what they wanted from artificial intelligence, but they could not figure out how to make computers learn and improve. The field reached a frustrating impasse that one scientific paper helped resolve.In 1986, scientists David Rumelhart, Geoffrey Hinton, and Ronald Williams developed backpropagation, a technique for training neural networks. Rather than programming the computers to follow all rules possible, they invented the technique of backpropagation. It enabled computers to learn from errors and made neural-network training far more practical.It is crucial to know how early AI systems were designed in order to grasp the significance of this breakthrough. Scientists attempted to simulate brain function using artificial neural networks. The networks are composed of layers of interconnected units that send information to each other, just as biological neurons do.However, the early models were very rigid. They could produce the desired result, but when the outcome was wrong, the model had no way to identify where the error came from. According to the history written by the Stanford HAI institute , early researchers could only change the settings of the last layer of the network.This was known as the credit assignment problem. When a multilayered network made an error, there was no way to distribute responsibility among the earlier hidden layers. As a result, the hidden layers could not be adjusted, and the deeper the network was, the less it could do.This 1986 paper solved the problem with an elegant idea. The authors showed that error information could be sent backward through the network so earlier layers could adjust their weights based on their contribution to the final error.To explain the idea in more general terms, the process may be viewed as a big team working on some complicated project. When the project turns out to be a failure, a manager using backpropagation is going to blame everyone who took part in it, rather than only the person who handed it over.The comprehensive review in Nature Reviews Neuroscience notes that this particular paper is considered to be the seminal one for this technique. According to the review, backpropagation became a key feature enabling learning in layered networks by systematically allocating credit and blame across layers. With this feedback, the network could learn increasingly complex concepts step by step without a designer fine-tuning every parameter.Although this particular paper provided a genius idea, the technology revolution around the world did not occur instantly. The transition happened slowly, more like a relay race than a burst. For many years, computers just did not have enough capacity and data to make layered neural nets work properly.However, what those scientists did in 1986 was introduce a powerful training method. They showed that layered neural networks could tackle complex nonlinear problems that earlier computers could not handle well. They introduced a dependable method for teaching machines to learn from examples.Many years down the road, as computer processors grew immensely faster and the internet brought in an immense amount of data, this theory finally met its match in practice. The same principle remains central to many modern deep learning systems, though implementations have evolved. In that sense, learning to move backward through a network helped make modern AI progress possible.
Article Summary: In 1986, scientists David Rumelhart, Geoffrey Hinton, and Ronald Williams developed backpropagation, a technique for training neural networks. It enabled computers to learn from errors and made neural-network training far more practical. The same principle remains central to many modern deep learning systems, though implementations have evolved.

Article Title: In 2014, Ian Goodfellow introduced competing neural nets that power modern AI image tools
Article Text:
StyleGAN2 This person does not actually exist. This image is of a fictitious person created by a generative adversarial network of type. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Creative competition between two machines

Why 2014 was the breakthrough year

A photo of American computer scientist Ian Goodfellow in Mountain View, California. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Overcoming the limitations of the code

In 2014, Ian Goodfellow proposed a concept that became influential in artificial intelligence. His notion revolved around two neural networks, which were computer structures designed like human brains, engaged in a never-ending battle against each other. One of the neural networks was responsible for creating images of its own imagination, and another one was trying to detect any lies. All of this was called a generative adversarial network, or GAN. This technique offered a new way for machines to improve through adversarial training. The concept is widely seen as an important milestone in modern image generation.At the heart of the invention of GAN lay a remarkably straightforward concept, considering the enormous implications it had. Rather than having humans develop detailed instructions for the machine to follow, the system learned through direct competition. The first model, called a generator, acts like a digital art forger trying to create convincing fakes. The second model, known as a discriminator, is an art detective that can spot the forgery among real images. This kind of rivalry results in internal motivation for improvement, with each failure of the detective serving as learning material for the forger. As a 2025 paper cited in PubMed indicates, the two-model architecture has become the common way to explain the functioning of GANs.This approach does not require the generator to understand image meaning in a human sense. It just needed to keep getting better at deceiving its opponent. Thus, the technique proved extremely helpful in creating realistic pictures regardless of the lack of training data available for the research. Over time, researchers found that the approach could be used for image-to-image translation, image enhancement, and synthetic data generation.Before Goodfellow's proposal, the development of deep learning was rapid, but computers were unable to produce believable pictures. The main problem with those earlier systems was that there was no way to refine their output. Generative Adversarial Networks provided a new solution to this challenge through the development of a gaming paradigm. In a 2026 review paper published in Nature , the researchers describe how the early versions of the networks developed the necessary framework for image generation techniques.Although more advanced technology has appeared since then, the idea continues to be an important landmark. The process was aimed at improving realism. It was due to the continuous struggle between the digital detective and the digital forger that the images produced became more realistic quickly. To cope with such challenges, the AI had to pay attention to small details.Nevertheless, GANs had many flaws that led to a new generation of AI development. Thus, according to the same review article published in Nature in 2026, initial GANs were often unstable in training and were plagued by the problem of mode collapsing. It occurs when the generator learns to produce one image that can fool the discriminator and repeats it endlessly.None of those shortcomings could negate the significance of the innovation. On the contrary, they helped researchers understand precisely where work was needed further on. After it became clear that it was possible to generate images realistically using GANs, other researchers were able to come up with new solutions, such as diffusion models, to overcome the issues of instability and low-quality results.Overall, the role of the 2014 proposal should be considered as that of a fantastic start instead of an ultimate conclusion. With his brilliant insight into the conflict of two computer programs, Ian Goodfellow managed to turn the complicated engineering task into a game. It was not just an innovative technological invention but also a breakthrough in terms of the philosophy of technology.
Article Summary: In 2014, computer scientist Ian Goodfellow proposed a concept that became influential in artificial intelligence. His notion revolved around two neural networks engaged in a never-ending battle against each other. One of the neural networks was responsible for creating images of its own imagination, and another one was trying to detect any lies. This technique offered a new way for machines to improve through adversarial training.

Article Title: In 1994, Denso engineers redesigning production labels unexpectedly invented the modern QR code
Article Text:
Masahiro Hara QR code's father honored by French artist qargo. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

An industry stuck in 1974: why are we still using 1D barcodes?

Why a square grid changed everything

Scan the QR Code to unlock instant information and access. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Built to survive the harsh reality of manufacturing

From car parts to a global standard

Today, the quick response code widely known as the QR code. We see it on restaurant tables to view menus, on digital tickets for concerts, and on smartphone screens to make quick payments. Because it is so common in our daily lives, it is easy to assume that it was invented by tech companies for modern smartphones. However, the true history of this black and white square is far less glamorous. It was developed out of frustration on a busy manufacturing floor in Japan.In 1994, engineers at a company called Denso Wave, which was a major parts supplier for the automotive giant Toyota, were facing a massive daily problem. The workers on the production line were struggling to keep track of thousands of car components moving through the factory. The old tools they relied on were simply failing to keep up with the speed of modern car manufacturing.Before the QR code existed, factories relied heavily on standard, one-dimensional barcodes. These are the familiar rows of vertical lines that you still see on grocery items today.While traditional barcodes worked well for simple tasks, they had severe technical limitations. A standard barcode can only hold about twenty alphanumeric characters of data.For the automotive workers at Denso Wave, twenty characters were not nearly enough. To track a single car part accurately, the label needed to include the part number, the batch details, the shipping destination, and manufacturing dates. Because a single barcode could not hold this much data, workers had to print several barcodes on a single part label. On the factory floor, employees spent a significant amount of time scanning up to fifteen separate barcodes on just one box of components. This process slowed production lines and caused wrist strain from repetitive scanning.To solve this production bottleneck, an engineer named Masahiro Hara led a small team to create a completely new type of tracking symbol. A research paper published by the University of Cincinnati states that the team decided to design a two-dimensional matrix code. This meant that instead of storing data in just one horizontal line of bars, the new code would store data both horizontally and vertically across a square grid.By utilizing two dimensions, the new design could hold several thousand characters of information in the exact same amount of physical space. In fact, it could store up to two hundred times more data than a standard barcode. This breakthrough meant that a single scan could reveal the key information a factory worker needed about a specific car part, reducing the need to scan multiple labels on a single box.A factory floor is a notoriously difficult environment for technology. Labels frequently get wiped with oil, covered in dust, or physically torn as parts are moved around by heavy machinery. Standard barcodes become completely unreadable if even a small part of their vertical lines is scratched or smudged.The engineers at Denso Wave designed the QR code to be durable. According to an industry analysis by the Polytechnic University of Catalonia , the matrix design allows the code to retain its data even when it is dirty or damaged. The code uses error correction technology, which means that up to thirty percent of the square can be destroyed or obscured and still be readable by a scanner. Furthermore, the distinct finder patterns in three corners of the code allow scanners to read it from any angle, whether it is upside down or tilted.What began as a clever piece of industrial engineering quickly outgrew the walls of the automotive assembly plant. Denso Wave made a highly unusual and generous decision at the time: they chose not to exercise their patent rights on the invention. They made the technology completely free for anyone in the world to use.This open access allowed the square code to slowly migrate from manufacturing plants into shipping logistics, retail, and eventually into the consumer market. When smartphones with built-in cameras became popular, the public suddenly had the power to read these codes instantly without any specialist industrial equipment. The simple square grid, originally built to survive oil stains and track car parts, became a bridge between the physical world and the digital one.
Article Summary: In 1994, engineers at a company called Denso Wave, which was a major parts supplier for the automotive giant Toyota, were facing a massive daily problem. The workers on the production line were struggling to keep track of thousands of car components moving through the factory. To solve this production bottleneck, an engineer named Masahiro Hara led a small team to create a new type of tracking symbol.

Article Title: In 1990, engineer Tim Berners-Lee developed a CERN browser that transformed online information access
Article Text:
Sir Timothy John Berners-Lee (born 8 June 1955), also known as TimBL, is an English computer scientist best known as the inventor of the World Wide Web, HTML, the URL system, and HTTP. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

A practical solution to the laboratory problem

NeXT workstation (NeXTcube and monitor) used by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN as the first Web server on the World Wide Web. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Why the browser changed everything

A

research tool

is becoming an everyday activity

Clicking on links, browsing pages, and exploring digital space is a matter of course for us now. But it did not happen by chance, and it was not simply the inevitable result of technological progress. In 1990, software engineer Tim Berners-Lee took the first steps on a project that would transform how people interact with information. He developed the project at CERN, the European Organisation for Nuclear Research.In reality, the World Wide Web was created not to build something new and unique, but to solve a practical problem within the research organization. As can be seen from the historical account of the Internet and the World Wide Web found on PubMed , the researchers of CERN have faced a great difficulty in sharing their documents and information about projects among different laboratories and departments.That timing is important for understanding the topic. This shows that the web browser did not emerge fully formed. Rather, it emerged from the need to connect documents and researchers within a readable network. In addition, the project itself was not aimed at creating a commercial product. It was meant to make the network easier for anyone in the laboratory to use.In order to make this new network possible, it became necessary to alter the documents themselves. Another peer-reviewed article on the history of computer languages explains how the foundation was laid. According to the article devoted to the history of SGML and XML languages, published on PubMed , HyperText Markup Language, better known as HTML, appeared at CERN in 1989.The development of HTML brought yet another important level of historical context to the browser itself. A browser is of limited use if the documents it opens do not connect. Unlike standalone computer files, HTML allowed one document to link to another. Moving between different pieces of information then became seamless. The web took shape in a way that users could navigate without understanding the underlying computer systems.Now that the language was ready, the system needed a way to access it. That way was provided by the browser itself. Historical accounts say the first text-based browser was developed in 1991, following the initial work in 1990. The browser was an integral part of the system, not an optional extra. Once users could open linked documents in the same interface, the web became a practical way to manage information.Before this, digital information was often stored in isolated systems. To access files, users often had to navigate separate systems and learn complex command structures. Hyperlinks changed this by creating a clear route from one webpage to another. Because HTML was publicly accessible, it spread quickly across the World Wide Web. Browsers provided access to web pages, while HTML supplied their content. The invention created a system that allowed people not only to store information but also to navigate it easily.One notable feature of the first browser was how simple it was. Being absolutely text-based, it had none of the sophisticated visual layouts, images, and other graphical elements that we see today. Yet that simplicity became an important advantage.Ultimately, the Internet became widely adopted because it made information feel more accessible. The change was not only about the application itself, but also about teaching people a new way to think about information. Web browsing turns reading into navigation. By turning a research tool into something routine, the early Internet helped create a more open and connected web.
Article Summary: Tim Berners-Lee is best known as the inventor of the World Wide Web, HTML, the URL system, and HTTP. He developed the project at CERN, the European Organisation for Nuclear Research. The web emerged from the need to connect documents and researchers within a readable network.

Article Title: India leads Asia Pacific in AI use: 60% of consumers want their own personal AI agents, says Adobe
Article Text:
Adobe representative image

AI is redefining the Indian shopping journey



65% use AI to search for personalized product recommendations.

62% are open to using a virtual AI concierge to handle their shopping.

60% rely on AI for instant customer service and troubleshooting.

The expectation gap



76% of consumers demand that AI-driven interactions feel human rather than robotic.

61% say they would completely stop doing business with a brand if they discovered they were talking to an AI when they expected a human.

Key Reassurances: Clear labeling that a tool is AI (21%) and the ability to instantly hand off the conversation to a human representative (17%) are the top demands for building consumer trust.

Indian consumers are showing the strongest appetite for advanced artificial intelligence (AI) in the Asia Pacific region, with a staggering 60% expressing a desire to build their own personal AI agents, a report has said. The findings, published on June 25 in the 16th annual Adobe AI and Digital Trends Report, reveal that India is overtaking both neighboring markets and local corporate expectations when it comes to embracing “agentic AI”, which are systems capable of executing complex tasks autonomously.The data highlights a cultural readiness for automated interactions. More than half of Indian consumers (55%) say they would happily interact with a brand’s AI agent, the report said, adding that 58% are comfortable with “agent-to-agent” interactions. Furthermore, 61% are perfectly comfortable letting an AI agent deal with a brand’s human representative on their behalf.Indian shoppers are increasingly integrating smart tools into their daily purchasing habits, looking for speed, convenience, and deep personalization. Adobe report says:“India is emerging as one of the most receptive markets for agentic AI in Asia Pacific, creating new opportunities for brands to deliver more personalised and effective customer experiences.At the same time, consumers expect these interactions to be transparent, intuitive and human-centric,” said Anindita Veluri, Director of Marketing at Adobe India.Despite high consumer enthusiasm, the report points to a disconnect between what everyday people want from AI and how companies measure its success. While brands remain focused on slashing costs and boosting internal efficiency metrics, consumers judge an AI's success on trust, transparency, and whether their needs are actually being met.“Many consumers are comfortable with agentic AI, but say adoption relies on defined transparent contexts with options for human support,” explained Duncan Egan, Adobe’s Vice President of Enterprise Marketing for Asia Pacific and Japan.The report notes that human connection still dictates whether a consumer stays or leaves:
Article Summary: More than half of Indian consumers (55%) say they would happily interact with a brand’s AI agent. 58% are comfortable with ‘agent-to-agent’ interactions. 61% are perfectly comfortable letting an AI agent deal on their behalf. Indian shoppers increasingly integrating smart tools into their daily purchasing habits.

Article Title: US and India locked in ‘sensitive’ national security talks over access to Anthropic’s Fable AI model
Article Text:
Us says it is following ‘gradual and measured’ approach for AI release



India seeks protections against ‘abrupt cutoffs’



The US and India are currently locked in high-level, “sensitive” national security discussions regarding the release and long-term reliability of advanced artificial intelligence (AI) models, including those developed by Anthropic, a report has said, adding that the ongoing dialogue has been confirmed by top officials from both nations.The talks come at a time when India is integrating AI into its digital ecosystem and governance systems, but there are fears that sudden export restrictions or geopolitical shifts could abruptly cut off access to vital foundational AI models.US Under Secretary of Economic Affairs Jacob Helberg said that Washington is prioritising a secure, highly collaborative approach to frontier AI technology with New Delhi. Speaking about the rollout of advanced systems like Anthropic's “Fable” model, Helberg stressed that the US is committed to a “gradual, measured approach” to ensure the software is safe for both countries and their allies.“We continue to have ongoing conversations about this topic with our Indian friends. These are very sensitive national security discussions that are not quite right for public consumption. But I think both sides really understand each other's perspectives.Our intention is very much to continue a gradual, measured approach to how we release Anthropic's models in a way that is safe... for our critical infrastructure, for our power grid,” Helberg said on the sidelines of the 2nd Pax Silica Summit in Washington, as per news agency ANI.When it comes to India, foundational AI technologies are being viewed as critical infrastructure, meaning sudden disruptions could paralyse long-term projects, public utilities and financial systems. S. Krishnan, Secretary of India's Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), explained that India explicitly raised concerns with the US regarding the long-term reliability of advanced models like Anthropic's Claude.These anxieties were heightened after the US Commerce Department issued an export control directive requiring Anthropic to restrict foreign nationals from using its newly launched Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models.“We sought an understanding of how exactly the US is looking at this particular aspect... because if it is something which is to be used and made available, we can't have abrupt cutoffs,” Krishnan stated.US officials have reportedly provided India with a clearer picture of their regulatory framework. According to Krishnan, the US gave assurances that it would work to ensure that access to crucial technology does not become a challenge for trusted global partners moving forward.
Article Summary: India and the US are locked in high-level national security discussions over the release and long-term reliability of advanced artificial intelligence (AI) models. The talks come at a time when India is integrating AI into its digital ecosystem and governance systems. There are fears that sudden export restrictions or geopolitical shifts could abruptly cut off access to vital AI models.

Article Title: IBM unveils world’s first technology for AI chips smaller than 1 nanometer
Article Text:
Breaking the boundaries of Moore’s Law



‘Nanostack’ 3D architecture: How IBM achieved this milestone



IBM has unveiled the world’s first technology capable of manufacturing microchips smaller than 1 nanometer. This comes as tech giants race to build more powerful hardware capable of handling increasingly massive artificial intelligence (AI) workloads.For decades, chipmakers have tried to squeeze more computing power into smaller spaces – a trend known as Moore’s Law. IBM’s new 0.7-nanometer (or 7 angstrom) architecture positions the company to compete with contract manufacturing giants like Intel and TSMC, as per a report by news agency Reuters.To put the achievement in perspective, Intel just recently announced that its 1.8-nanometer process had entered “risk production” testing. IBM's new technology shrinks components even further, packing nearly 100 billion transistors onto a surface the size of a fingernail. Compared to IBM’s milestone 2-nanometer chip released in 2021, this new architecture is claimed to offer 50% higher computing performance or 70% greater energy efficiency.To achieve sub-1-nanometer sizes, IBM developed a proprietary transistor design called “nanostack”. Instead of laying transistors flat next to each other on a chip, the nanostack design layers them vertically in three dimensions.“With our new nanostack architecture, we’re not just making smaller transistors, we’re reinventing how chips are built to deliver dramatically more power and energy efficiency,” said Jay Gambetta, director of IBM Research.Beyond raw processing power, the nanostack technology tackles a critical bottleneck in AI computing: memory. The new process shrinks static random-access memory (SRAM) circuits by a massive 40%. This specific type of memory is highly coveted by the AI industry, and is utilised in Nvidia's new Groq chips and hardware from Cerebras Systems – both of whom currently rely entirely on TSMC for manufacturing.IBM estimates that commercial production using this technology could begin within the next five years. While IBM designs these cutting-edge blueprints, it does not operate its own commercial factories. The company has previously licensed its chip innovations to global manufacturers like Samsung and Japan's Rapidus.
Article Summary: IBM has unveiled the world’s first technology capable of manufacturing microchips smaller than 1 nanometer. This comes as tech giants race to build more powerful hardware capable of handling increasingly massive artificial intelligence (AI) workloads. The new architecture is claimed to offer 50% higher computing performance or 70% greater energy efficiency.
